1185	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1186	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1187	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1188	  left-whiskered morphism
1189	  \[
1190	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1191	  \]
1192	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1193	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1194	
1195	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1196	  \emph{full} instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the
1197	  load-bearing field of the off-path \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal-category
1198	  path (arbitrary \(F\)). Its content for an arbitrary \(F\) is exactly the live lemma
1199	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, which the associator's
1200	  sheafification transport consumes and which is closed \emph{unconditionally} by the
1201	  d.2 stalk--tensor commutation (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}); the d.1/d.2
1202	  ingredients are therefore on the critical path. The two proofs recorded below --- a
1203	  sheaf-level argument specialised to locally trivial \(F\), and the general stalkwise
1204	  argument via (d.1)/(d.2) --- are retained for the historical record; the live group
1205	  law closes this obligation in its general (d.2) form through
1206	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.
1207	\end{lemma}
1208	
1209	\begin{proof}
1210	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1211	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1212	
1213	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1214	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1215	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1216	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1217	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1218	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1219	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1220	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1221	  On such a \(V\):
1222	  \begin{itemize}
1223	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1224	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1225	      \[
1226	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1227	      \]
1228	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1229	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1230	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1231	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1232	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1233	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1234	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1235	  \end{itemize}
1236	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1237	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1238	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1239	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1240	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1241	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1242	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1243	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1244	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1245	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.
1246	
1247	  This is \emph{not} the section-level route that tensors a bare section map \(g(V)\)
1248	  --- only injective --- and stalls on a \(\mathrm{Tor}_1\)/flatness obligation. Here
1249	  the argument is sheaf-level and uses that \(g\) is locally \emph{bijective} (a
1250	  \(J.W\) morphism), reduced through the unitor to \(g|_V\) (again a \(J.W\)
1251	  morphism); a mere injection is never tensored, so no flatness is needed.
1252	
1253	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1254	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1255	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1256	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1257	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1258	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1259	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1260	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1261	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1262	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1263	  \[
1264	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1265	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1266	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1267	  \]
1268	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1269	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1270	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1271	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1272	
1273	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1274	  pinned commit:
1275	  \begin{enumerate}
1276	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1277	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1278	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1279	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1280	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1281	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1282	      above.
1283	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1284	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1285	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1286	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1287	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1288	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1289	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1290	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1291	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1292	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1293	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1294	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1295	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1296	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1297	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1298	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1299	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1300	  \end{enumerate}
1301	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1302	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1303	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1304	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1305	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1306	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1307	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1308	
1309	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1310	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1311	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1312	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1313	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1314	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1315	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1316	\end{proof}

1318	\emph{Superseded route --- off path, not to be formalized.} The following lemma
1319	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1320	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1321	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1322	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).
1323	This block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be
1324	formalized.

1326	\begin{lemma}
1327	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1328	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1329	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1330	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1331	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1332	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1333	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1334	  \(J.W\):
1335	  \[
1336	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1337	    \qquad
1338	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1339	  \]
1340	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1341	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1342	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1343	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1344	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1345	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1346	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1347	\end{lemma}
1348	
1349	\begin{proof}
1350	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1351	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1352	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1353	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1354	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1355	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1356	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1357	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1358	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1359	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1360	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1361	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1362	\end{proof}

1367	needed. The group law consumes only the existence of the associator, unitor and
1368	braiding isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, assembled by hand
1369	rather than off a coherent monoidal category's \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)), and the
1370	associator is now built directly by

1372	block is retained for the historical record only and must \emph{not} be formalized.

1374	\begin{lemma}
1375	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1376	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1377	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1378	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1379	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1380	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1381	  instance
1382	  \[
1383	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1384	  \]
1385	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1386	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1387	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1388	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1389	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1390	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1391	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1392	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1393	  category
1394	  \[
1395	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1396	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1397	  \]
1398	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1399	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1400	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1401	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1402	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1403	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1404	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1405	  composites.
1406	
1407	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it depends on the sole open obligation
1408	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1409	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1410	\end{lemma}
1411	
1412	\begin{proof}
1413	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1414	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1415	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1416	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1417	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1418	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1419	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1420	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1421	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1422	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1423	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1424	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1425	  at any point.
1426	\end{proof}

1428	\begin{lemma}
1429	
1430	
1431	\leanok
1432	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1433	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1434	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1435	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1436	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1437	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1438	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1439	  \[
1440	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1441	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1442	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1443	  \]
1444	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1445	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1446	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1447	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1448	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1449	  ever required.
1450	
1451	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1452	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1453	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1454	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1455	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1456	\end{lemma}
1457	
1458	\begin{proof}
1459	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1460	        lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}
1461	  \textbf{Realization (sheafification transport, unconditional via d.2).} The
1462	  associator is the sheafification transport of the presheaf-of-modules associator
1463	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\), which exists for
1464	  \emph{all} presheaves of modules over the varying ring. The transport requires the
1465	  single left-whisker \(F \triangleleft \mathtt{toSheafify}\) to lie in the
1466	  sheafification localizer \(J.W\) for the relevant \(F\); that single open
1467	  left-whiskering obligation \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) is
1468	  closed \emph{unconditionally} --- for an arbitrary whiskering object, with no
1469	  flatness and no local triviality --- by the d.2 stalk--tensor commutation
1470	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk} (\cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}):
1471	  a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, and by the d.2 commutation
1472	  \(\bigl(F \triangleleft g\bigr)_x \cong \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) is again an
1473	  isomorphism, so \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). Hence the associator is itself
1474	  unconditional and axiom-clean once d.2 (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}) is
1475	  formalized; it does \emph{not} require the full \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
1476	  or the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category --- only the one left-whisker
1477	  lemma --- and no sectionwise flatness enters. The local-gluing description below
1478	  records the equivalent concrete local realization through the
1479	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1480	
1481	  Concretely, the same isomorphism is described by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
1482	  through the restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1483	  Fix a
1484	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1485	  composite
1486	  \[
1487	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1488	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1489	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1490	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1491	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1492	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1493	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1494	  \]
1495	  in which:
1496	  \begin{itemize}
1497	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1498	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1499	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1500	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1501	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1502	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1503	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1504	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1505	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1506	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1507	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1508	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1509	  \end{itemize}
1510	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1511	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1512	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1513	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1514	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1515	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1516	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1517	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1518	  composites agree on the overlap.
1519	
1520	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1521	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1522	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1523	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1524	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1525	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1526	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1527	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1528	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1529	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1530	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1531	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1532	
1533	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1534	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1535	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1536	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1537	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1538	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1539	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1540	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1541	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1542	  specialisation can replace it. What this proof does \emph{not} require is the
1543	  \emph{full} \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance or the
1544	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) monoidal category
1545	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}): only the single left-whisker obligation
1546	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}, closed via the d.2 stalk--tensor
1547	  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, is consumed by the sheafification
1548	  transport. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1549	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1550	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1551	\end{proof}

1613	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
1614	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}

1627	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1628	  % "\begin{lemma}
1629	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
1630	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1631	  % ...
1632	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
1633	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
1634	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}

1638	  % \begin{definition}
1639	  % \label{definition-pic}
1640	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1641	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
1642	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
1643	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
1644	  % corresponding to tensor product.
1645	  % \end{definition}"
1646	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1647	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction
1648	  is an isomorphism) and Definition~\texttt{definition-pic} (the Picard group is
1649	  the abelian group of iso-classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules under
1650	  tensor product).}
1651	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1652	  bundle. The dual
1653	  \[
1654	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1655	  \]
1656	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1657	  isomorphism
1658	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1659	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1660	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1661	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1662	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1663	\end{lemma}
1664	
1665	\begin{proof}
1666	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1667	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
1668	  \textbf{Infrastructure-blocked.} This statement is \emph{not} realizable with the
1669	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module infrastructure presently available: its construction
1670	  depends on a \emph{sheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules}
1671	  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\), an object that does not yet
1672	  exist at the presheaf, sheaf, or general-categorical level (there is no
1673	  monoidal-closed / internal-hom / dual structure on
1674	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) or \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), and no object-level
1675	  descent assembling a global \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module from local pieces). The dual
1676	  object \(L^{-1}\) therefore cannot even be \emph{named} until that infrastructure
1677	  is built. The construction of the required internal hom, its dual, and its
1678	  evaluation is decomposed into individually-formalizable sub-steps in
1679	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} through
1680	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}); the present lemma is realized once those land,
1681	  and its Lean body is a placeholder until then.
1682	
1683	  We record the intended mathematical route, all three steps of which are correct
1684	  modulo the missing object. \emph{The route is as follows.} Take
1685	  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) to be the
1686	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, and let
1687	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) be the evaluation of
1688	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}.
1689	
1690	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1691	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1692	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1693	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1694	  \[
1695	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1696	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1697	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1698	  \]
1699	  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
1700	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1701	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1702	
1703	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1704	  morphism
1705	  \[
1706	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1707	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1708	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1709	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1710	  \]
1711	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1712	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1713	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1714	  \[
1715	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1716	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1717	  \]
1718	  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
1719	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1720	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1721	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1722	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1723	
1724	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1725	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1726	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1727	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1728	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1729	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1730	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1731	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1732	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1733	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).
1734	
1735	  Consequently, \emph{once \(L^{-1}\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) are available from
1736	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}}, every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor
1737	  inverse \(L^{-1}\) under \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of
1738	  line bundles is an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1739	  The local-triviality of \(L^{-1}\) (Step~1) is \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} and
1740	  the local-iso\(\Rightarrow\)global-iso passage (Step~3) is the already-closed
1741	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the single non-available input is the dual object
1742	  and its evaluation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
1743	\end{proof}

1789	\begin{lemma}
1790	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1791	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1792	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1793	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1794	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1795	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1796	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1797	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1798	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1799	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1800	  functor
1801	  \[
1802	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1803	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1804	  \]
1805	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1806	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1807	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1808	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1809	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1810	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1811	  canonical isomorphism
1812	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1813	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1814	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1815	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.

1817	
1818	\begin{proof}
1819	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1820	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1821	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1822	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1823	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1824	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1825	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1826	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1827	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1828	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1829	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1830	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1831	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1832	  same cover.

1835	\section{The varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) and the unconditional
1836	left-whiskering}
1837	\label{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor}
1838	
1839	This section supplies the one genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient on which the
1840	\emph{unconditional} associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} rests: the commutation
1841	of the stalk functor with the relative-ring tensor product of presheaves of modules
1842	(``d.2''). Together with the already-built \(R_x\)-linear stalk comparison
1843	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, ``d.1'') it closes the single open left-whiskering
1844	obligation for an \emph{arbitrary} whiskering object --- with no flatness and no
1845	local-triviality hypothesis --- which is exactly what makes the sheafification
1846	transport of the presheaf associator work for all modules. The construction is to be
1847	formalized in the dedicated Lean file
1848	\(\mathtt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor.lean}\).
1849	
1850	\begin{lemma}
1851	[Stalk of a tensor product (d.2): varying-ring stalk--tensor commutation]
1852	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}
1853	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorIso}
1854	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1855	  % NOTE (iter-234): partial — stages (i)–(ii) of the proof are formalized: the
1856	  % section-level balanced bilinear descent (`stalkTensorBilin`, `stalkTensorDescU`)
1857	  % and the FORWARD additive comparison map
1858	  % `PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDesc : (A ⊗ᵖ B).stalk x ⟶ A_x ⊗_{R_x} B_x`
1859	  % (axiom-clean, germ characterisation `stalkTensorDesc_germ_tmul`), in
1860	  % StalkTensor.lean. The pinned full iso `stalkTensorIso` does NOT yet exist:
1861	  % stage (iii) `R_x`-linearity is blocked on the CommRingCat/RingCat carrier-duality
1862	  % bridge (`stalkTensorDescU_smul`), stage (iv) is the reverse map by nested colimit
1863	  % descent, stage (v) is the germ-generator mutual inversion. Hence this block stays
1864	  % unmarked.
1865	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Tensor product),
1866	  % Lemma lemma-stalk-tensor-product (stalk of a tensor product of modules is the
1867	  % tensor product of the stalks over the stalk ring)
1868	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L2332-L2344).
1869	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1870	  % "\begin{lemma}
1871	  % \label{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}
1872	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
1873	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
1874	  % Let $x \in X$. There is a canonical isomorphism
1875	  % of $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$-modules
1876	  % $$
1877	  % (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})_x
1878	  % =
1879	  % \mathcal{F}_x \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{X, x}} \mathcal{G}_x
1880	  % $$
1881	  % functorial in $\mathcal{F}$ and $\mathcal{G}$.
1882	  % \end{lemma}"
1883	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1884	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-stalk-tensor-product}.}
1885	  Let \(R : (\mathtt{Opens}\,X)^{op} \to \mathtt{CommRingCat}\) be the structure
1886	  presheaf of a scheme \(X\), and let \(A, B\) be presheaves of
1887	  \(R\)-modules (objects of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ
1888	  \mathtt{forget_2}\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat})\)). For each point
1889	  \(x \in X\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \((R \circ
1890	  \mathtt{forget_2}).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-modules
1891	  \[
1892	    (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1893	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1894	    A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
1895	  \]
1896	  natural in \(A\) and \(B\), where \(\otimes^{p}\) is the presheaf-of-modules tensor
1897	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1898	\end{lemma}
1899	
1900	\begin{proof}
1901	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1902	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1903	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1904	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1905	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1906	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1907	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages, in the order in which it is built;
1908	  stages (i)--(ii) (the forward additive comparison map and its germ
1909	  characterisation) are already in place, and stages (iii)--(v) upgrade it to an
1910	  \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual inversion.
1911	
1912	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1913	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\); done). For a fixed
1914	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1915	  \[
1916	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1917	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1918	  \]
1919	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1920	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1921	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1922	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1923	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1924	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1925	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1926	  \[
1927	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1928	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1929	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1930	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1931	  \]
1932	
1933	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1934	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\); done). The
1935	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1936	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1937	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1938	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1939	  forward additive comparison map
1940	  \[
1941	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1942	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1943	  \]
1944	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1945	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1946	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1947	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1948	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1949	
1950	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1951	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1952	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1953	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1954	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1955	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1956	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1957	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1958	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1959	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1960	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) only fires after a small
1961	  \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\)/\(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) bridge between the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
1962	  and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers is inserted. Once that bridge is in place the
1963	  linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern already used for the
1964	  d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1965	
1966	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1967	  \[
1968	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1969	  \]
1970	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1971	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1972	  \[
1973	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1974	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1975	  \]
1976	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1977	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1978	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1979	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1980	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1981	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1982	  neighbourhood system, and \(R_x\)-bilinearity reduces to the germ--scalar
1983	  compatibility of (i)/(iii).
1984	
1985	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
1986	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
1987	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
1988	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
1989	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
1990	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
1991	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
1992	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
1993	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
1994	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
1995	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
1996	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
1997	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
1998	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
1999	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
2000	
2001	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
2002	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
2003	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
2004	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit
2005	  ring'' identification together with the carrier-duality bridge of (iii). Conceptually
2006	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
2007	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
2008	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
2009	  inverse.
2010	\end{proof}

2047	  morphism \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\) is \(J\)-locally injective
2048	  (indeed lies in \(J.W\)). No flatness and no local-triviality hypothesis on \(F\) is
2049	  required. This discharges the open left-whiskering obligation

2051	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes.

2055	% above and the stalkwise criterion for membership in the sheafification localizer;

2057	\begin{proof}
2058	  \uses{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation, lem:stalk_linear_map}
2059	  A morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules lies in \(J.W\) (is locally bijective) iff
2060	  it is a stalkwise isomorphism: this is the ``d.1'' packaging --- the
2061	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) characterisation combined with
2062	  \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf.isIso\_iff\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on the topological
2063	  site \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\). Since \(g \in J.W\), each stalk map \(g_x\) is an
2064	  isomorphism. By the d.2 commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, the stalk
2065	  of the whiskered morphism is identified with
2066	  \[
2067	    (F \triangleleft g)_x \;\cong\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x
2068	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \longrightarrow F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is an isomorphism because any functor (here \(F_x \otimes_{R_x} -\)) preserves
2071	  isomorphisms. Thus \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, hence lies in
2072	  \(J.W\) by d.1 again, and in particular is \(J\)-locally injective
2073	  (\(\mathtt{GrothendieckTopology.W.isLocallyInjective}\)). No flatness, no local
2074	  triviality, and the whiskering object \(F\) is arbitrary.
2075	\end{proof}

2077	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
2078	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
2079	
2080	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
2081	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
2082	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2083	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
2084	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
2085	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
2086	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
2087	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
2088	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
2089	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
2090	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
2091	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
2092	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
2093	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

2213	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit

2216	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2217	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
2218	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
2219	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
2220	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
2221	  01CR).
2222	
2223	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
2224	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
2225	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
2226	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
2227	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2228	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
2229	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
2230	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2231	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
2232	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
2233	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
2234	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
2235	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
2236	
2237	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
2238	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
2239	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
2240	  \[
2241	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
2242	    \quad
2243	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
2244	    \quad
2245	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
2246	    \quad
2247	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
2248	  \]
2249	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
2250	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2251	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
2252	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
2253	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
2254	  any of the four axiom proofs.

2256	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
2257	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
2258	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
2259	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
2260	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
2261	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
2262	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
2263	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
2264	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
2265	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
2266	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
2267	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
2268	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
2269	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
2270	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take

2278	  pinned commit.

2280	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
2281	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
2282	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
2283	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
2284	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
2285	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and the
2286	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The associator
2287	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is supplied by the unconditional route of
2288	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_stalk_tensor} and transitively invokes the d.2 stalk--tensor
2289	  commutation \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}.
2290	
2291	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
2292	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
2293	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
2294	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
2295	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
2296	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
2297	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
2298	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
2299	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
2300	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
2301	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
2302	  already supplies.
2303	\end{lemma}
2304	
2305	\begin{proof}
2306	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2307	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2308	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2309	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
2310	  Work directly on isomorphism classes of locally-trivial sheaves, as in Mathlib's
2311	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2312	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
2313	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
2314	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2315	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
2316	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
2317	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
2318	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
2319	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
2320	  read as an equality of iso-classes,
2321	  \[
2322	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
2323	    \quad
2324	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
2325	    \quad
2326	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
2327	    \quad
2328	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle\beta\rangle:
2329	  \]
2330	  associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors
2331	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2332	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
2333	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
2334	  This is the same one-isomorphism-per-law shape that Mathlib's
2335	  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
2336	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}) uses on a skeletal
2337	  monoidal category via its skeletality map \(\mathtt{hC}\), but we do \emph{not}
2338	  instantiate that construction: it requires a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\)
2339	  (and \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), which is
2340	  exactly the structure for the varying structure sheaf that is unavailable.
2341	  Finally every element is invertible: the dual \(L^{-1}\) of
2342	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong
2343	  \mathcal{O}_X\), so \([L^{-1}]\) is a two-sided inverse of \([L]\); equivalently,
2344	  \([L]\) is a unit precisely when \(L\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
2345	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried
2346	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). This assembles the abelian group ---
2347	  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}
2348	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2349	  exactly as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) on
2350	  iso-classes of invertible modules (there off a fixed-ring \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2351	  here by hand).
2352	
2353	  Alternatively (fallback), once \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} provides the full
2354	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) via
2355	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), the group is the units of the skeleton,
2356	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\), exactly the
2357	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
2358	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.
2359	\end{proof}

2361	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2362	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2363	
2364	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2365	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2366	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2367	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2368	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2369	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2370	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2371	
2372	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2373	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2374	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2375	\[
2376	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2377	  \;:\equiv\;
2378	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2379	\]
2380	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2381	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2382	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2383	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2384	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2385	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2386	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2387	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2388	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).
2389	
2390	\begin{lemma}
2391	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on invertible objects (corollary of the unconditional
2392	associator)]
2393	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2394	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2395	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2396	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2397	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
2398	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2399	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2400	  \[
2401	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2402	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2403	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2404	  \]
2405	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The invertibility-carrier group law
2406	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2407	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2408	  \cong M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality
2409	  coherence is ever required.
2410	\end{lemma}
2411	
2412	\begin{proof}
2413	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
2414	  Immediate specialisation of the \emph{unconditional} associator
2415	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to the invertible arguments \(M, N, P\); no
2416	  hypothesis on the arguments is used. (The earlier route argued via ``invertible
2417	  \(\Rightarrow\) locally free \(\Rightarrow\) sectionwise flat'' to feed the
2418	  flat-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}; that step is \emph{false}
2419	  --- local freeness does not give global sectionwise flatness \(M(U)\) over
2420	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) for a non-affine open \(U\) --- and is no longer needed, since
2421	  the general associator is itself unconditional, its single open left-whiskering
2422	  obligation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} being closed via the d.2
2423	  stalk--tensor commutation \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whiskerleft_via_stalk}.)
2424	\end{proof}

2426	\begin{definition}
2427	[The invertibility-carrier Picard group \(\Pic X\)]
2428	  \label{def:pic_carrier}
2429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.PicGroup}
2430	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}
2431	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2432	  % modules), Definition definition-pic (the Picard group as the abelian group of
2433	  % iso-classes of invertible modules under tensor product)
2434	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4350-L4357).
2435	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2436	  % "\begin{definition}
2437	  % \label{definition-pic}
2438	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2439	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2440	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2441	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2442	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2443	  % \end{definition}"
2444	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}:
2445	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) is the abelian group of isomorphism classes of
2446	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, with addition the tensor product.}
2447	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. Consider the subtype of \(\otimes\)-invertible objects
2448	  \[
2449	    \mathtt{Invertible}\,X
2450	    \;:=\;
2451	    \bigl\{\, M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
2452	              \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M \,\bigr\}
2453	  \]
2454	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}, and equip it with the setoid whose
2455	  relation is ``there exists an isomorphism'',
2456	  \(M \sim M' :\equiv \mathrm{Nonempty}(M \cong M')\) (a proposition-valued
2457	  equivalence relation: reflexive, symmetric and transitive because isomorphisms
2458	  compose and invert). The \emph{Picard group carrier}
2459	  \[
2460	    \Pic X \;:=\; \mathtt{Invertible}\,X \,/\!\sim
2461	  \]
2462	  is the quotient type of isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
2463	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. We write \([M] \in \Pic X\) for the class of an
2464	  invertible \(M\). This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2465	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\), carried on \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) rather than on
2466	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\); on a scheme the two predicates agree (Stacks tag
2467	  0B8M), but only the invertibility form carries the inverse witness existentially.
2468	\end{definition}

2518	    \;\cong\;

2607	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}

2609	  with isomorphisms in place of equalities. Chain the unitor
2610	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the two hypotheses, the associator
2611	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the braiding
2612	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:

2617	    \;\cong\; (N \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N'
2618	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X N'
2619	    \;\cong\; N',

2622	  third is the associator, the fourth uses the braiding

2624	  the unitors. Hence \(N \cong N'\). For well-definedness on classes, note that if
2625	  \(M \cong M'\) then a tensor inverse of \(M\) is also a tensor inverse of \(M'\)

2659	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2660	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2661	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2662	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2663	  % \end{definition}"
2664	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}
2665	  and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}: the isomorphism classes of
2666	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules form an abelian group under tensor product,
2667	  with unit \(\mathcal{O}_X\).}
2668	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The carrier \(\Pic X\) of \cref{def:pic_carrier} is an
2669	  abelian group, with
2670	  \[
2671	    [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes_X M'],
2672	    \qquad
2673	    1 := [\mathcal{O}_X],
2674	    \qquad
2675	    [M]^{-1} := [N] \ \text{for any witness } M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X.
2676	  \]
2677	\end{theorem}
2678	
2679	\begin{proof}
2680	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
2681	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2682	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2683	  Each group-operation datum and each axiom is supplied by a single
2684	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).
2685	
2686	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
2687	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
2688	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
2689	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2690	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
2691	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
2692	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
2693	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
2694	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
2695	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
2696	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
2697	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
2698	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
2699	
2700	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
2701	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
2702	  \[
2703	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
2704	    \quad
2705	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
2706	    \quad
2707	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
2708	    \quad
2709	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
2710	    \quad
2711	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
2712	  \]
2713	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
2714	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
2715	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
2716	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
2717	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
2718	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
2719	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
2720	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
2721	  for a two-sided inverse.
2722	
2723	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
2724	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
2725	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
2726	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
2727	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
2728	  thereby off this group's critical path.
2729	\end{proof}
2730	
2731	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
2732	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
2733	
2734	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
2735	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
2736	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
2737	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2738	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
2739	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
2740	
2741	\begin{theorem}
2742	
2743	
2744	\leanok
2745	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
2746	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
2747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
2748	  % NOTE (carrier pivot, deferred): the \uses below still points at the old
2749	  % locally-trivial group law lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup; this is DEFERRED and
2750	  % will be repointed to thm:pic_commgroup (the invertibility-carrier group, inverse
2751	  % free) once that carrier group is formalized. Left unchanged this iter.
2752	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2753	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2754	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2755	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2756	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2757	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2758	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
2759	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2760	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2761	
2762	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
2763	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
2764	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
2765	  % groups)
2766	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2767	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2768	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2769	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2770	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2771	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2772	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2773	  % \end{dfn}
2774	  %  [\dots]
2775	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2776	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2777	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2778	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2779	  % topologies  by
2780	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2781	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2782	  %  \end{dfn}"
2783	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2784	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2785	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2786	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2787	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2788	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2789	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2790	  \[
2791	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2792	  \]
2793	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2794	  abelian-group structure with addition
2795	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2796	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2797	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2798	  inverse
2799	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2800	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2801	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2802	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2803	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2804	  map
2805	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2806	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2807	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2808	\end{theorem}
2809	
2810	\begin{proof}
2811	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2812	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2813	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2814	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2815	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2816	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2817	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2818	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2819	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2820	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2821	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2822	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2823	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2824	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2825	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2826	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2827	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2828	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2829	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2830	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2831	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2832	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2833	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2834	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2835	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2836	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2837	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2838	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2839	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2840	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2841	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2842	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2843	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2844	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2845	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2846	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2847	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2848	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2849	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2850	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2851	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2852	
2853	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2854	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2855	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2856	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2857	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2858	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2859	
2860	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2861	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2862	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2863	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2864	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2865	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2866	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2867	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2868	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2869	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2870	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
